title: The Kingdom of Mali and the City of Timbuktu

caps_topic: The kingdom of Mali and the city of Timbuktu

sources.title: Grade 7 - Term 1: The Kingdom of Mali and the City of Timbuktu in the 14th Century
Summary: The Kingdom of Mali and the City of Timbuktu in the 14th Century

slides.title: The Kingdom of Mali and the City of Timbuktu
slides.lead: A wealthy West African empire built on gold and salt - and a desert city that became one of the great centres of learning in the medieval world.

media.alt: A golden sun rising over sand dunes with a caravan of camels crossing the desert.

slides.notes: Open by placing Mali on a map of West Africa (near the Niger River, in the region of present-day Mali, Senegal and Guinea). Ask learners what they already imagine when they hear 'empire' or 'trade'. Establish the two big ideas of this lesson: (1) how control of gold and salt made Mali rich and powerful, and (2) how the city of Timbuktu became famous across Africa and the Islamic world as a centre of learning. Remind learners this happened mostly in the 1200s to 1500s, long before European colonisers arrived.

slides.title: Where and when was Mali?
slides.lead: Mali grew up along the Niger River in West Africa and was one of the largest empires of its time.

blocks.text: The Mali Empire lasted from about **1235 to 1600**. At its height it stretched from the **Atlantic coast** in the west across the grasslands (the Sahel) to the edge of the **Sahara Desert** in the north and east.

items: It covered parts of what are today Mali, Senegal, Guinea and neighbouring countries.
The great **Niger River** gave people water, fish and a route for trade by boat.
Its capital was **Niani**, a busy town near the gold-producing lands.

media.alt: A simple map of West Africa with a river winding through it and the empire shaded in the middle.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 170' role='img'><rect width='240' height='170' fill='#cfe3ec'/><path d='M14 40 h150 l40 20 v70 l-40 20 H14 Z' fill='#e6dcc0'/><path d='M14 40 h150 l40 20 v18 H14 Z' fill='#e9c98a'/><path d='M0 60 q20 20 60 24 q40 4 70 30 q30 24 70 20' fill='none' stroke='#3d78a8' stroke-width='4'/><circle cx='120' cy='96' r='5' fill='#8a1f1f'/><text x='128' y='100' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='11' fill='#3a3a3a'>Niani</text><text x='150' y='58' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='10' fill='#7a5a1a'>Sahara</text></svg>

slides.notes: Help learners orient themselves. This is West Africa, not southern Africa, and the events are roughly 800 years ago. Point out the Sahel - the belt of dry grassland between the desert and the wetter forests to the south. Explain that Mali sat perfectly between two things people badly needed from each other: gold from the south and salt from the desert north. That in-between position is the key to the whole lesson.

slides.title: Sundiata Keita, the founder
slides.lead: Mali began when a leader named Sundiata Keita united the people and won a great victory.

blocks.text: Around **1235**, **Sundiata Keita** defeated a rival ruler, Sumanguru Kante, and brought many smaller chiefdoms together into one kingdom. Sundiata is remembered as a wise and brave founder-king.

items: His story is kept alive by **griots** - trained storytellers who pass history down by word of mouth.
The griots' account of Sundiata is one of Africa's most famous oral epics.
This reminds us that history can be recorded in **memory and spoken word**, not only in writing.

media.alt: A storyteller sitting with a stringed instrument in front of listeners.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='118' width='220' height='42' fill='#d8b877'/><circle cx='70' cy='60' r='16' fill='#7a5230'/><rect x='56' y='76' width='28' height='42' rx='6' fill='#2f5233'/><ellipse cx='96' cy='104' rx='16' ry='20' fill='#c9a24b'/><line x1='96' y1='84' x2='96' y2='124' stroke='#5c4326' stroke-width='3'/><g fill='#8a1f1f'><circle cx='150' cy='72' r='10'/><circle cx='178' cy='70' r='10'/><rect x='142' y='84' width='16' height='30' rx='4'/><rect x='170' y='82' width='16' height='32' rx='4'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: Introduce oral history through the griots (also called jeli). This is a strong CAPS teaching point: not all reliable history is written down. Explain that griots memorised long family and royal histories and recited them at ceremonies. The 'Epic of Sundiata' was passed down this way for centuries before it was written. Invite learners to compare this with how their own family or community history is remembered and retold.

slides.title: Gold from the south, salt from the north
slides.lead: Mali grew rich by controlling the trade between two things people could not live without.

columns.text: Rich **gold mines** lay to the south of Mali. Gold was used as money and was wanted across North Africa, Europe and the Middle East.
The king was so powerful that, by law, all large **gold nuggets belonged to him**, while ordinary people could trade in gold dust.
Salt was mined in the **Sahara Desert** to the north. In the hot climate people needed salt to stay healthy and to keep food from going bad.
Salt was so scarce in the south that it was sometimes traded for **almost its own weight in gold**.

slides.notes: This is the heart of the lesson: the 'gold-salt trade'. Make the exchange vivid - people in the goldfields had gold but no salt; people in the desert had salt but no gold, so each side wanted what the other had. Mali sat in the middle and taxed the goods passing through, which is how the rulers became wealthy without mining gold or digging salt themselves. Emphasise the surprising idea that salt could be as precious as gold.

slides.title: Trade across the Sahara
slides.lead: Goods travelled thousands of kilometres by camel caravan across the desert.

blocks.heading: Camels
blocks.text: Camels can travel long distances without water and cope with the desert's heat, so they were the perfect pack animals.

blocks.heading: Caravans
blocks.text: Traders travelled together in large caravans - often around a thousand camels, sometimes far more - for safety.

blocks.heading: Trade goods
blocks.text: Gold, ivory and kola nuts went north; salt, cloth, books and glass came south into Mali.

media.alt: A line of camels carrying loads across desert dunes.

slides.notes: Explain why the Sahara was such a barrier and why the camel was the technology that made trans-Saharan trade possible - historians sometimes call the camel the 'ship of the desert'. Note that traders were mostly Muslim merchants from North Africa, and along with goods they carried ideas, the Arabic language and the religion of Islam. Trade was not only about wealth; it connected Mali to a wider world.

slides.title: Mansa Musa
slides.lead: Mansa Musa ruled Mali in the early 1300s and is often called one of the richest people in history.

blocks.text: '**Mansa**' means 'king' or 'emperor'. Mansa Musa ruled at the height of Mali's power. Under him the empire was peaceful, well-organised and famous far beyond Africa for its **gold**.

items: He was a **Muslim** ruler who encouraged learning, mosques and schools.
He made Mali known in North Africa, the Middle East and Europe.
He brought back scholars, books and architects to build up his cities.

media.alt: A crowned ruler seated on a throne holding a golden nugget.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 200 160' role='img'><rect width='200' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='70' y='70' width='60' height='70' rx='4' fill='#8a1f1f'/><rect x='62' y='60' width='76' height='16' rx='3' fill='#5c1414'/><circle cx='100' cy='52' r='16' fill='#7a5230'/><path d='M82 40 l6 -14 l6 10 l6 -12 l6 12 l6 -10 l6 14 Z' fill='#e8a13a'/><circle cx='128' cy='96' r='9' fill='#e8a13a'/></svg>

slides.notes: Introduce the meaning of 'Mansa' so the title is not mistaken for a name. Set up the next slide (his famous journey) by stressing that Mansa Musa's wealth was so great it amazed people in distant lands. Be careful and age-appropriate: 'richest person in history' is a memorable claim, but it is an estimate made much later - present it as 'often said to be' rather than a proven fact. This models good historical thinking about sources.

slides.eyebrow: A journey that made Mali famous
slides.title: The pilgrimage to Mecca, 1324
slides.lead: Mansa Musa's journey across Africa to the holy city of Mecca showed the whole world how wealthy Mali was.

blocks.text: As a Muslim, Mansa Musa made the **hajj** - the pilgrimage to **Mecca** - in about **1324**. He travelled with a huge company of people and large amounts of gold, giving gifts along the way.
The king of Mali travelled to Mecca with a great company and so much gold that news of his wealth spread across the world.
blocks.cite: Based on medieval chroniclers' accounts of Mansa Musa's hajj

items: He gave away and spent so much gold in **Egypt** that its value there is said to have dropped for years.
After his journey, Mali began to appear on **European maps** of the world.
The pilgrimage linked Mali more closely to the wider Muslim world of trade and learning.

slides.notes: Explain the hajj simply as a religious duty for Muslims to travel to Mecca. Use this slide to connect wealth, religion and reputation. The story that Mansa Musa's gold caused prices in Cairo to fall is repeated by many historians and is a great example of cause and effect. [SME] The exact dates and figures (the year 1324, the size of his company, the length of the price disruption) come from later chroniclers and vary between sources - present them as widely reported rather than precise. Emphasise the lasting result: Mali became known across the medieval world.

slides.eyebrow: The city on the desert's edge
slides.title: Timbuktu, a great trading city
slides.lead: Timbuktu grew where the Niger River met the desert trade routes, making it a natural meeting place.

blocks.text: **Timbuktu** sat near the point where river trade met the desert caravans. Goods, people and ideas from many places came together there, and the city became rich and busy under Mali's rule.

items: Traders exchanged gold, salt, cloth, and many other goods in its markets.
Beautiful **mosques** were built there, some from mud brick that still stand today.
Timbuktu is now a **UNESCO World Heritage Site**, protected for its history.

media.alt: A mud-brick mosque with wooden beams sticking out and a tall tower.

slides.notes: Show why location made Timbuktu special: it was the meeting point of two transport systems, the river and the desert caravan. Point out the distinctive mud-brick (adobe) architecture with wooden beams, such as the Djinguereber Mosque built in Mansa Musa's time. Mention that these buildings are fragile and have needed protection, which is why Timbuktu is a UNESCO World Heritage Site - a good link to the idea of heritage and why we preserve the past.

slides.title: Timbuktu, the city of scholars
slides.lead: Timbuktu became one of the most important places in Africa to study and learn.

blocks.text: Around its mosques, especially **Sankore**, grew schools and universities. Students came from across Africa to study **religion, law, mathematics, astronomy and medicine**.

items: At its busiest, thousands of students studied in the city at one time.
Teachers were respected scholars, and learning brought honour to the city.
Timbuktu became a symbol of African **knowledge and education**.

media.alt: Students seated around a teacher, with open books between them.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='124' width='220' height='36' fill='#d8b877'/><circle cx='110' cy='52' r='16' fill='#7a5230'/><rect x='96' y='68' width='28' height='40' rx='6' fill='#2f5233'/><g fill='#8a1f1f'><circle cx='56' cy='84' r='11'/><rect x='46' y='96' width='20' height='26' rx='5'/><circle cx='164' cy='84' r='11'/><rect x='154' y='96' width='20' height='26' rx='5'/></g><rect x='84' y='112' width='24' height='8' fill='#e8e0cc' stroke='#5c4326'/><rect x='112' y='112' width='24' height='8' fill='#e8e0cc' stroke='#5c4326'/></svg>

slides.notes: This slide addresses the CAPS focus on Timbuktu as a centre of learning. Correct a common misconception: medieval Africa had cities of scholars and libraries, not just villages. The Sankore mosque-university was the best known. [SME] Student numbers are often quoted as around 25,000, but this figure comes from later estimates and should be treated as approximate. Ask learners why a place famous for wealth might also become famous for learning - wealth and trade attracted scholars and paid for books and schools.

slides.title: The manuscripts of Timbuktu
slides.lead: Timbuktu's scholars wrote and collected hundreds of thousands of handwritten books.

blocks.text: Books were copied by hand in **Arabic** and other languages and treasured by families for generations. So many were made that, at times, **trading in books was one of the city's most profitable businesses**.

items: The manuscripts covered science, history, poetry, trade and faith.
They are proof that West Africa had a rich **written history**, not only oral tradition.
South Africa later helped preserve these manuscripts through the **Timbuktu project**.

media.alt: An open handwritten manuscript with lines of script and a decorated border.

slides.notes: Use the manuscripts to challenge the myth that Africa had 'no history' before Europeans wrote about it. Timbuktu's libraries hold tens of thousands - by some counts hundreds of thousands - of manuscripts. [SME] Total manuscript counts vary widely between sources (from the tens of thousands into the hundreds of thousands) and should be given as a broad range. This is a proud South African connection: after 2001 South Africa supported a project to build an archive and help conserve the Timbuktu manuscripts, which teachers can mention to link the topic to learners' own country.

slides.title: Mali and Timbuktu through time

blocks.alt: A timeline of the Mali Empire from about 1235 to the 1600s.

events.label: Sundiata Keita founds the Mali Empire
Mansa Musa rules at the empire's height
Great mosque built at Timbuktu; learning grows
The Songhai Empire has replaced Mali as the great power

slides.notes: Use the timeline to consolidate the story so far and to practise working with dates and centuries - a key CAPS skill. Point out that these are approximate ('c.' means 'about') because much of the record comes from oral tradition and later writers. Ask learners to place the pilgrimage within Mansa Musa's reign and to see how long the empire lasted - well over 300 years.

slides.title: The end of Mali - but not its story
slides.lead: Mali slowly weakened, but its ideas, trade routes and city of learning outlived the empire.

blocks.text: After the 1300s, quarrels over power, attacks by desert peoples and the rise of the neighbouring **Songhai Empire** slowly broke Mali apart. By around **1600** Songhai and others controlled the old trade routes and cities.

items: Timbuktu remained a centre of trade and learning under new rulers.
Mali's story shows Africa had powerful, wealthy and well-organised states.
Today we study Mali to understand Africa's own rich past.

media.alt: The sun setting behind dunes, with a mosque tower in the distance.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 150' role='img'><rect width='240' height='150' fill='#f6e2c2'/><circle cx='120' cy='70' r='34' fill='#e8a13a'/><path d='M0 96 q60 -20 120 0 t120 0 v54 H0 Z' fill='#c98a4b'/><path d='M0 116 q80 -14 160 0 t80 0 v34 H0 Z' fill='#a86f36'/><rect x='190' y='60' width='12' height='44' fill='#7a5230'/><path d='M191 60 l5 -10 l5 10 Z' fill='#5c4326'/></svg>

slides.notes: Close the historical narrative and draw out the significance. The main teaching point is change and continuity: the Mali Empire fell, but the trade routes and Timbuktu's role as a place of learning continued under Songhai. End on the big CAPS idea for the whole topic: precolonial Africa had large, wealthy and sophisticated states with cities, trade networks and centres of scholarship. This helps learners value Africa's own history.

slides.title: What we learned
slides.lead: Mali grew rich by controlling the gold-salt trade, and Timbuktu became a famous centre of learning.

blocks.text: We saw how **Sundiata Keita** founded Mali, how **Mansa Musa** made it famous, how the **gold-salt trade** created its wealth, and how **Timbuktu** became a city of scholars and books. Now test what you remember.

blocks.question: Explain how the trade in gold and salt made the kingdom of Mali wealthy.
Why did Mansa Musa's pilgrimage to Mecca make Mali famous in other parts of the world?
Describe two reasons why Timbuktu became known as an important centre of learning.

slides.notes: Use the three questions as a written task or class discussion; the mark allocations follow the CAPS style, mixing recall with explanation. Encourage learners to answer in full sentences and to use the key words from the lesson (Sahel, caravan, hajj, Sankore, manuscript, griot). Remind them of the overarching lesson: precolonial West Africa was home to a wealthy, powerful empire and a world-famous city of learning.
